verificationNote: Sector-specific requirements are constructed from IEC 62443, BACnet/IP (ASHRAE 135), and NACSA Act 854 obligations for building automation systems in Malaysia. Covers government buildings, data centres, hospitals, airports, and commercial high-rises. These requirements have not been endorsed by JKR, CIDB, or NACSA.
nacsaSectorLead: JKR (Jabatan Kerja Raya) for government buildings
nacsaSectorNumber: Subset of Government and ICT sectors for government buildings
applicableStandards: NIST SP 800-188 (De-identification of IoT data — relevant for smart building analytics)
sectorOtCharacteristics: BACnet/IP, KNX, and LON protocols were designed for interoperability, not security — most lack authentication and encryption
BMS is often managed by facilities management (FM) companies with limited cybersecurity expertise
BMS networks frequently interconnected with corporate IT for energy management dashboards and remote monitoring
Data centre BMS cooling failure is an availability threat to critical IT infrastructure — thermal shutdown cascades
Hospital BMS compromise can affect sterile environments (operating theatres, clean rooms) and patient safety
Fire alarm and suppression systems are life-safety — false activation causes evacuation disruption; suppression failure during real fire is catastrophic
Smart building IoT devices (occupancy sensors, lighting, energy meters) create large attack surface with minimal security

requirements.name: BACnet/KNX protocol security
requirements.description: BACnet/IP is the dominant building automation protocol. Standard BACnet/IP has no authentication — any device on the same network can read/write BACnet objects (setpoints, schedules, alarms). BACnet Secure Connect (BACnet/SC) adds TLS encryption and authentication but adoption is limited. KNX similarly lacks authentication in its basic form. Protocol-level vulnerabilities mean network segmentation is the primary security control.

legal.summary: BACnet/IP and KNX are OT protocols in building automation. If BMS is part of NCII-designated infrastructure (government buildings, data centres), NACSA s18 security measures apply. ASHRAE 135 Addendum BJ defines BACnet Secure Connect for new installations.
legal.owner: BMS Engineer / Facilities Manager / OT Security

technical.summary: BMS on dedicated network, isolated from corporate IT. BACnet traffic confined to BMS VLAN via firewall. BACnet/SC (TLS) for new installations where controller firmware supports it. For legacy BACnet/IP: compensating controls are network isolation and monitoring.
actions: BMS network on dedicated VLAN(s) — BACnet/IP and KNX traffic must not traverse corporate IT network
Firewall between BMS network and corporate IT: allow only specific, documented data flows (energy dashboards, alarm notifications) via BMS gateway/proxy in IDMZ
BACnet/SC (Secure Connect) for all new BMS installations: TLS 1.2+ encryption and certificate-based device authentication
Legacy BACnet/IP systems: compensating controls are network isolation (dedicated VLAN), OT monitoring for anomalous BACnet commands, and physical access control to BMS controllers
KNX installations: KNX Secure (Data Secure and IP Secure) for new installations; legacy KNX — physical media isolation (twisted pair not accessible from public areas)
Disable BACnet broadcast (Who-Is/I-Am) at VLAN boundaries to prevent BACnet device discovery from outside BMS network
BMS web interfaces (Honeywell, Siemens, Johnson Controls): change default credentials; enforce HTTPS; restrict access to BMS engineering VLAN only

governance.summary: BMS protocol security reviewed at each major BMS upgrade. BACnet/SC migration roadmap maintained.
actions: BACnet/SC migration plan: timeline for replacing legacy BACnet/IP controllers with BACnet/SC-capable devices at next replacement cycle
Annual BMS network architecture review — verify isolation from corporate IT
BMS default credential audit: verify no default passwords remain on any BMS controller or web interface

evidenceItems: BMS network architecture diagram (VLAN isolation from corporate IT)
BMS firewall rules (only documented flows permitted)
Legacy BACnet/IP exception register with compensating controls

slMapping.sl1: BMS on dedicated VLAN; default credentials changed; basic firewall between BMS and IT
slMapping.sl2: BACnet traffic confined to BMS VLANs; BMS gateway/proxy in IDMZ; BMS web interfaces HTTPS-only; BACnet broadcast contained
slMapping.sl3: BACnet/SC on new installations; OT monitoring on BMS network; legacy exceptions documented; annual architecture review

requirements.name: HVAC system isolation and data centre cooling protection
requirements.description: HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) systems maintain environmental conditions for occupant comfort, clean room compliance (hospitals, semiconductor fabs), and critical equipment cooling (data centres). Data centre cooling failure can cause thermal shutdown of servers within minutes, making BMS an availability dependency for critical IT infrastructure. HVAC setpoint manipulation could also waste energy or create uncomfortable/unsafe conditions.
applicableZones: Hospital HVAC and clean room controls

legal.summary: Data centre BMS is a critical dependency for NCII-designated IT infrastructure. HVAC failure causing data centre thermal shutdown is an indirect NCII incident. Hospital HVAC affecting clean rooms has patient safety implications.
legal.owner: Facilities Manager / Data Centre Manager / OT Security

technical.summary: HVAC controllers on dedicated BMS segment. Data centre cooling systems have independent temperature monitoring with hardware-based high-temperature alarms. No single point of failure for data centre cooling control. HVAC setpoint changes logged and alerted.
actions: Data centre HVAC controllers on dedicated BMS segment — isolated from general building BMS where possible
Independent temperature monitoring in data centre: hardware-based high-temperature alarms that trigger regardless of BMS status (independent sensors, direct-wired to alarm panel)
HVAC setpoint changes: log all changes (who, when, old value, new value); alert facilities team on setpoint changes outside maintenance windows

Data centre cooling redundancy: N+1 minimum; BMS failure must not disable all cooling units simultaneously — verify that BMS loss-of-communication causes cooling units to maintain last known setpoint (fail-safe)
Hospital clean room HVAC: differential pressure monitoring with independent sensors; pressure deviation alerts independent of BMS
Remote access to HVAC controllers: via BMS engineering workstation only; no direct internet access to HVAC controllers; MFA for remote BMS access

governance.summary: Data centre BMS security included in data centre certification/audit scope. HVAC fail-safe behaviour tested annually.
actions: Annual HVAC fail-safe test: verify data centre cooling units maintain operation if BMS communication is lost
Data centre BMS included in ISO 27001 / SOC 2 audit scope (where applicable)
Hospital HVAC clean room compliance: BMS integrity included in clean room qualification

evidenceItems: Data centre HVAC network isolation diagram
HVAC fail-safe test records (loss of BMS communication test)

slMapping.sl2: HVAC on dedicated BMS segment; independent high-temp alarms; setpoint change logging; fail-safe verified
slMapping.sl3: Data centre HVAC isolated from general BMS; independent differential pressure monitoring (hospital); MFA for remote BMS access; annual fail-safe testing
slMapping.sl4: Real-time anomaly detection on HVAC parameters; cryptographic integrity of HVAC controller programs; automated response to anomalous setpoint changes

requirements.name: Physical access control system (PACS) integration security
requirements.description: Building Physical Access Control Systems (PACS — card readers, biometric scanners, door controllers) are increasingly integrated with BMS for unified building management. PACS compromise could allow physical access bypass (doors unlocked remotely), disable intrusion detection, or be used for physical surveillance. PACS often integrate with IT identity management (Active Directory) creating a cross-domain attack path.

legal.summary: PACS processes personal data (biometric data, access logs) — PDPA Act 709 applies. PACS integrated with BMS creates a cross-domain security boundary. For government buildings and NCII facilities, PACS integrity is a physical security dependency.
legal.owner: Physical Security Manager / BMS Engineer / OT Security

technical.summary: PACS on dedicated network segment, separated from general BMS. PACS-to-BMS integration via controlled interface only (elevator dispatch, lighting zones). PACS controller database encrypted and backed up. Door fail-safe behaviour defined per zone.
actions: PACS controllers on dedicated network segment — separated from general BMS VLAN even if same physical infrastructure
PACS-to-BMS integration: defined interfaces only (e.g., occupancy-based HVAC/lighting); PACS must not expose door unlock commands on BMS network
PACS-to-IT integration (Active Directory): via dedicated integration server in IDMZ; PACS controller not directly joined to AD domain
PACS controller database: encrypted at rest; backed up daily; backup stored separately from PACS server
Door fail-safe behaviour: fire-rated doors fail-unlock on fire alarm (life safety); secure areas fail-lock on PACS failure (security); behaviour documented per door/zone
PACS audit trail: all access events (grant, deny, alarm, override) logged with timestamp; logs retained per PDPA and organisational requirements
Biometric data (fingerprint, facial recognition): encrypted in transit and at rest; access restricted to PACS system only; not exported to BMS or IT systems

governance.summary: PACS security included in physical security audit scope. PDPA compliance for biometric data documented.
actions: Annual PACS security assessment — verify network isolation, fail-safe behaviour, and audit trail integrity
PDPA compliance review for PACS biometric data: data protection impact assessment (DPIA) for biometric processing
PACS administrator access review: quarterly review of PACS admin accounts

evidenceItems: PACS network architecture diagram (separate from general BMS)
Biometric data protection evidence (encryption, access restriction)

slMapping.sl2: PACS on dedicated segment; fail-safe behaviour defined; audit trail enabled; biometric data encrypted; daily backup
slMapping.sl3: PACS-IT integration via IDMZ; PACS door commands not exposed on BMS network; quarterly admin review; annual security assessment
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic integrity of PACS controller configuration; real-time anomaly detection on access patterns; tamper-evident PACS controller hardware

requirements.name: Fire and life safety system protection
requirements.description: Fire alarm and suppression systems (fire detection panels, sprinkler control, gas suppression, smoke management) are life-safety systems. Compromise could disable fire detection (masking real fires), trigger false alarms (disrupting operations and causing evacuation), or activate suppression in occupied areas. Fire systems are typically standalone but increasingly integrated with BMS for monitoring and smoke management coordination.

legal.summary: Fire alarm and suppression systems must comply with UBBL and Fire Services Act. Cybersecurity compromise that affects fire system operation is a life-safety incident. Fire system integrity is a prerequisite for building occupancy permit.
legal.owner: Fire Safety Manager / Facilities Manager / OT Security

technical.summary: Fire alarm and suppression systems on dedicated, isolated network — not connected to BMS control network. BMS may receive fire alarm status via read-only interface for monitoring and smoke management coordination. No BMS commands to fire system. Fire panel has independent power supply.
actions: Fire alarm and suppression system on dedicated network — physically separate from BMS control network where possible; at minimum, separate VLAN with no BMS write access
BMS-to-fire system interface: read-only for alarm status monitoring and smoke management coordination; BMS cannot send commands to fire system (arm, disarm, test, suppress)
Fire alarm panel: standalone operation independent of BMS; if BMS communication fails, fire system must continue operating autonomously
Fire system engineering access: physical key access at fire panel; no remote programming of fire alarm panel via network
Gas suppression systems (FM-200, Novec, CO2): manual hold-off switches at protected room entries; suppression activation in occupied data centres requires manual confirmation
Fire system integrity: periodic loop testing per Fire Services Act requirements; cyber security assessment of any network-connected fire system components
UPS and battery backup for fire panel: independent of building UPS; tested per maintenance schedule

governance.summary: Fire system security integrated into fire safety management. Annual fire system inspection includes cybersecurity review of network-connected components.
actions: Annual fire system cybersecurity review: verify network isolation, read-only BMS interface, and autonomous operation capability
Fire system change management: any modification to fire system programming or network connectivity requires fire safety manager approval
Fire system incident response: any suspected cyber compromise of fire system triggers immediate fire safety assessment

evidenceItems: Fire system network architecture (isolation from BMS documented)
Fire system autonomous operation test records

slMapping.sl2: Fire system on dedicated network; read-only BMS interface; physical key access for programming; autonomous operation verified
slMapping.sl3: Fire system physically separate from BMS network; annual cybersecurity review; gas suppression manual confirmation for occupied spaces; fire system change management
slMapping.sl4: Continuous integrity monitoring of fire system configuration; tamper detection on fire panel; independent fire system health monitoring separate from BMS

requirements.name: Smart building IoT security
requirements.description: Smart building initiatives deploy large numbers of IoT devices (occupancy sensors, air quality monitors, smart lighting, energy meters, digital signage) connected to BMS or dedicated IoT platforms. These devices have limited security capabilities (weak authentication, no encryption, infrequent patching) and create a large attack surface. Compromised IoT devices can be used as network pivot points to reach higher-value BMS controllers.

legal.summary: Smart building IoT devices processing occupancy or environmental data may fall under PDPA if linked to identifiable individuals. IoT as an attack surface for BMS requires IEC 62443 zone segmentation.
legal.owner: Smart Building Manager / IT/OT Security / Facilities

technical.summary: IoT devices on dedicated IoT VLAN, isolated from BMS control network and corporate IT. IoT gateway aggregates data and presents to BMS/analytics via controlled interface. No direct IoT-to-BMS controller communication. Default credentials changed on all IoT devices.
actions: IoT devices on dedicated VLAN — completely isolated from BMS control VLAN and corporate IT network
IoT gateway: aggregates IoT sensor data; presents to BMS or cloud analytics via IDMZ interface; IoT devices do not communicate directly with BMS controllers
Default credential elimination: change all default passwords on IoT devices at deployment; maintain credential register
IoT device firmware updates: establish update process (OTA or manual); track firmware versions; apply security patches within 30 days of vendor release
IoT device inventory: maintain asset register of all deployed IoT devices including firmware version, location, and network address
Network access control (802.1X or MAC-based): only registered IoT devices permitted on IoT VLAN; rogue device detection
IoT data privacy: if occupancy or environmental sensors can be linked to individuals, PDPA compliance required; minimise data collection to operational need

governance.summary: IoT security policy defines deployment, maintenance, and decommissioning requirements for smart building devices.
actions: IoT security policy: defines minimum security requirements for smart building IoT devices before deployment approval
IoT asset inventory reviewed quarterly — decommission devices no longer needed; verify firmware currency
PDPA assessment for new IoT deployments that collect occupancy or presence data

evidenceItems: IoT network architecture diagram (dedicated VLAN isolation)
IoT device asset register (with firmware versions)
Network access control configuration for IoT VLAN
PDPA assessment for IoT data collection (where applicable)

slMapping.sl1: IoT on dedicated VLAN; default credentials changed; device inventory maintained
slMapping.sl2: IoT gateway in IDMZ; no direct IoT-to-BMS controller paths; firmware update process; network access control
slMapping.sl3: Rogue device detection on IoT VLAN; automated firmware vulnerability scanning; PDPA compliance for occupancy data; quarterly inventory review

requirements.name: Elevator and escalator control system security
requirements.description: Elevator control systems are increasingly network-connected for destination dispatch optimisation, remote monitoring, predictive maintenance, and integration with PACS (access-controlled floors). Compromise could strand passengers between floors, override safety interlocks, or enable unauthorised floor access. Escalator drive controllers can be manipulated to cause sudden stops or speed changes that endanger passengers. Elevator safety circuits (overspeed governors, door interlocks, buffer switches) must operate independently of network-connected control systems.

legal.summary: Lifts Act 1984 and DOSH regulations mandate safety requirements for elevators. Safety circuits must not be compromisable via network. Remote monitoring connections must not create safety vulnerabilities.
legal.owner: Facilities Manager / Elevator Maintenance Contractor / OT Security

technical.summary: Elevator controllers on dedicated BMS sub-segment. Safety circuits (overspeed, door interlock, buffer) hardware-based and independent of network. Remote monitoring via read-only gateway. PACS-elevator integration via defined interface only.
actions: Elevator controllers on dedicated BMS VLAN — not on same segment as general BMS controllers

Safety circuits (overspeed governor, door interlocks, final limit switch, buffer switches) must be hardware-based and independent of any network-connected control system — no software override possible via network
Destination dispatch system: access controlled; floor assignment changes logged; PACS integration via defined read-only interface for access-controlled floors
Remote monitoring: vendor remote monitoring via read-only data export through IDMZ gateway; no remote command capability to elevator controller from internet
Elevator controller firmware updates: vendor-approved only; tested on single elevator before fleet deployment; scheduled during low-traffic periods
Escalator drive controllers: emergency stop circuits hardware-wired; speed control parameters protected from unauthorised network modification

governance.summary: Elevator OT security reviewed as part of Lifts Act compliance inspection. Vendor access documented.
actions: Annual elevator cybersecurity review aligned with Lifts Act inspection schedule
Elevator vendor remote access log maintained and reviewed quarterly
Elevator safety circuit independence verified during annual safety inspection

evidenceItems: Safety circuit independence evidence (hardware safety documentation)

slMapping.sl2: Elevator controllers on dedicated VLAN; safety circuits hardware-independent; remote monitoring read-only; vendor access logged
slMapping.sl3: PACS-elevator integration via defined interface; firmware update testing on single unit first; annual cybersecurity review; escalator emergency stop hardware-wired
slMapping.sl4: Continuous monitoring of elevator controller communications; cryptographic integrity of elevator controller firmware; anomaly detection on destination dispatch patterns

requirements.name: Lighting control and emergency lighting security
requirements.description: Modern intelligent lighting systems use DALI, KNX, or IP-based protocols for schedule-based control, occupancy-based dimming, and daylight harvesting. Emergency lighting is a life-safety system that must illuminate evacuation routes during power failure or fire. Compromise of lighting control could cause darkness (safety hazard, panic), energy waste, or disable emergency lighting during an emergency. Smart lighting systems with occupancy sensors can also be used for surveillance (tracking building occupancy patterns).

legal.summary: Emergency lighting is mandated by UBBL and Fire Services Act. Emergency lighting must operate independently of BMS and lighting control systems. Occupancy sensor data may have PDPA implications.
legal.owner: Facilities Manager / Electrical Engineer / OT Security

technical.summary: General lighting control on BMS network. Emergency lighting on independent system with battery backup — not dependent on BMS or lighting control network. DALI/KNX protocols have no inherent security — compensating controls via network isolation.
actions: General lighting control system (DALI/KNX/IP) on dedicated BMS VLAN or sub-segment
Emergency lighting: completely independent system with dedicated battery backup; must operate regardless of BMS, lighting control, or mains power status; not controllable from BMS
Emergency lighting central monitoring: monitoring connection read-only; BMS may receive emergency lighting status but cannot command emergency lighting
DALI/KNX protocol security: these protocols have no authentication; compensating controls are physical access restriction to DALI/KNX bus wiring and network isolation
IP-based lighting gateways: change default credentials; restrict management access to BMS engineering VLAN; firmware updates applied regularly
Occupancy sensor data: if used for analytics beyond lighting control, assess PDPA compliance; minimise data retention

governance.summary: Emergency lighting security reviewed with fire safety compliance. Lighting control default credentials audited.
actions: Emergency lighting independence verified during annual fire safety inspection
Lighting control default credential audit annually
Occupancy sensor data use reviewed for PDPA compliance

slMapping.sl1: Lighting control on BMS VLAN; emergency lighting independent; default credentials changed
slMapping.sl2: IP lighting gateways secured; emergency lighting monitoring read-only; DALI/KNX physical access restricted; firmware updates current
slMapping.sl3: Occupancy sensor PDPA compliance; emergency lighting independence formally verified; lighting control anomaly monitoring

requirements.name: BMS integration platform and central server security
requirements.description: The central BMS integration platform (Honeywell EBI, Siemens Desigo CC, Johnson Controls Metasys, Schneider EcoStruxure) is the highest-value target in building automation. It aggregates control of all building subsystems — HVAC, fire alarm, PACS, elevator, lighting, energy — into a unified management interface. Compromise of the central BMS server potentially gives an attacker access to all integrated subsystems. BMS web and mobile interfaces are increasingly common, expanding the attack surface to include web application vulnerabilities.

legal.summary: Central BMS server is the primary OT asset for building automation security. For NCII-designated buildings, BMS server integrity is a core NACSA obligation. BMS web interfaces are subject to web application security requirements.
legal.owner: BMS Administrator / Facilities Manager / IT Security / OT Security

technical.summary: Central BMS server on dedicated server segment. BMS web interface behind reverse proxy with MFA. Role-based access control with least privilege. Integration with subsystems via defined BACnet/OPC interfaces only. BMS database encrypted and backed up.
actions: Central BMS server on dedicated network segment — not on general IT server infrastructure
BMS web interface: behind reverse proxy in IDMZ; HTTPS enforced; multi-factor authentication for all BMS web access; session timeout configured
BMS mobile application: if used, same authentication requirements as web interface; mobile device management (MDM) required for devices accessing BMS

Role-based access control: least privilege principle; operators see/control only their assigned building systems; supervisors approve setpoint changes outside normal ranges; administrators manage user accounts and system configuration
BMS database: encrypted at rest; daily backup to separate storage; backup integrity verified monthly
BMS-to-subsystem integration: defined BACnet/OPC interfaces only; BMS cannot override safety system logic (fire alarm, elevator safety circuits)
BMS audit trail: all operator actions logged with user ID, timestamp, action, old value, new value; audit trail retained per organisational policy (minimum 12 months)
BMS server hardening: remove unnecessary services; disable unused BACnet services; restrict management ports to authorised workstations

governance.summary: Central BMS security reviewed annually. BMS access control reviewed quarterly. BMS included in building security audit scope.
actions: Annual BMS server security assessment — vulnerability scan and configuration review
BMS user access review quarterly — remove stale accounts; verify role assignments
BMS change management: any changes to BMS server configuration, integration interfaces, or user roles require change management approval
BMS vendor support access: documented; time-limited; monitored

evidenceItems: BMS web interface security configuration (reverse proxy, MFA, HTTPS)
BMS database backup and encryption configuration
BMS audit trail configuration and sample

slMapping.sl2: BMS server on dedicated segment; HTTPS and MFA for web access; RBAC; database encrypted and backed up; audit trail enabled
slMapping.sl3: Reverse proxy in IDMZ; mobile access via MDM; subsystem integration restricted to defined interfaces; quarterly access review; annual security assessment
slMapping.sl4: Continuous BMS server integrity monitoring; real-time anomaly detection on operator commands; cryptographic integrity of BMS configuration; automated detection of configuration drift

subsectors.name: HVAC Systems
subsectors.description: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning systems control environmental conditions for occupant comfort, clean room compliance, and equipment cooling. Critical for data centres, hospitals, and semiconductor fabs.
securityLevels: Dedicated BMS VLAN for HVAC controllers
Independent temperature monitoring for critical spaces
Setpoint change logging and alerting
Fail-safe to last-known-good on communication loss

subsectors.name: Fire and Life Safety Systems
subsectors.description: Fire detection, alarm, and suppression systems are life-safety critical. False activation causes evacuation disruption; failure during real fire is catastrophic. Systems must operate autonomously regardless of BMS status.
securityLevels: Physically separate network from BMS
Read-only BMS interface for monitoring only
Autonomous operation independent of BMS
Physical key access for programming

subsectors.name: Physical Access Control Systems
subsectors.description: Card readers, biometric scanners, door controllers, and turnstiles. PACS compromise enables physical access bypass. Integration with BMS and IT identity systems creates cross-domain attack paths.
securityLevels: Dedicated PACS network segment
PACS-IT integration via IDMZ only
Biometric data encrypted at rest and in transit
Door fail-safe behaviour defined per zone

subsectors.name: Elevator and Escalator Control
subsectors.description: Elevator and escalator control systems manage vertical transportation in buildings. Modern elevator systems are network-connected for destination dispatch, remote monitoring, and predictive maintenance. Compromise could strand passengers, cause safety incidents, or disrupt building operations.
securityLevels: Elevator controller on dedicated VLAN
Safety circuits hardware-independent of network
Remote monitoring via read-only gateway
Vendor maintenance access controlled and logged

subsectors.name: Lighting Control Systems
subsectors.description: Intelligent lighting control systems manage lighting schedules, occupancy-based dimming, daylight harvesting, and emergency lighting. DALI, KNX, and IP-based lighting protocols are common. Emergency lighting is life-safety critical and must operate independently.
securityLevels: Lighting network on dedicated VLAN
Emergency lighting independent of BMS
Default credentials changed on all controllers

subsectors.name: BMS Integration Platform
subsectors.description: The central BMS integration platform aggregates all building subsystems (HVAC, fire, access, elevator, lighting, energy) into a unified management interface. This is the highest-value target in building automation as compromise provides control over all integrated subsystems.
otSystems: Central BMS server (Honeywell EBI, Siemens Desigo CC, Johnson Controls Metasys)
securityLevels: BMS servers in dedicated network segment
BMS web interface with HTTPS and MFA
Role-based access control
Integration with subsystems via defined interfaces only
No direct internet access to BMS server

sectorSpecificThreats.threat: Data centre cooling failure from BMS compromise (IT availability cascade)
Physical access control bypass via PACS exploit
Fire suppression system disabling or false activation
IoT botnet pivot into BMS control network
